Current qualification status of the IP core against the target process node and foundry: not_started, in_progress (characterization underway), qualified (passed all qualification criteria), failed (qualification failed, remediation required). Distinct from silicon_proven status.

Date on which this version of the IP core was officially released and made available for design-in use in the internal IP library or vendor catalog. Used for version history tracking and design project planning.

Indicates whether the IP core and its associated physical implementation materials comply with the EU RoHS Directive restricting hazardous substances (True = RoHS compliant; False = non-compliant or not assessed). Required for products sold in the EU market.

business_glossary_term: Intellectual Property (IP) Core Royalty Rate Percentage
description: Per-unit royalty rate expressed as a percentage of the net selling price of the end product incorporating this IP core. Applicable only for royalty_bearing license types. Used for royalty accrual and financial reporting in SAP S/4HANA.

business_glossary_term: Register Transfer Level (RTL) Hardware Description Language
description: Hardware description language used for the RTL source code of the IP core (Verilog, SystemVerilog, VHDL, or mixed). Relevant only for soft IP and firm IP types. Determines EDA tool compatibility and synthesis flow requirements.
Percentage of internal logic nodes covered by scan-based DFT test structures, as reported by ATPG tools. Higher coverage reduces DPPM risk. Null if DFT is not applicable (e.g., analog IP).

Indicates whether this IP core has been successfully validated in a fabricated silicon device (True = silicon-proven; False = simulation-only or pre-silicon). Silicon-proven status is a critical risk factor in IP selection for production designs.
Date on which the IP core achieved silicon-proven status through successful tape-out and functional validation in fabricated silicon. Null if not yet silicon-proven.

Indicates whether the IP core originates from the internal IP development team, a third-party vendor (e.g., ARM, Synopsys DesignWare, Cadence Tensilica), or an open-source library. Determines licensing obligations, support model, and NRE cost applicability.

Level of technical support available for this IP core: standard (community/documentation support), premium (dedicated FAE support with SLA), end_of_support (no active support, use at own risk). Influences design-in risk assessment and vendor engagement planning.

Indicates whether Liberty (.lib) timing models are available for this IP core for static timing analysis (True = Liberty models provided; False = not available). Required for timing closure analysis in Synopsys PrimeTime and Cadence Tempus.

Timestamp when this IP core record was most recently modified in the master catalog (yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX). Supports change tracking, data lineage, and incremental ETL processing in the Databricks Lakehouse.

Name of the third-party IP vendor or licensor (e.g., ARM Holdings, Synopsys, Cadence Design Systems, CEVA). Null for internally developed IP cores. Used for vendor management, license tracking, and procurement.
Version identifier of the IP core release following semantic versioning (e.g., 2.1.0, r0p4). Tracks revision history and ensures design projects reference the correct IP release.
Unique surrogate identifier for each IP core integration instance record within a design project. Primary key for the ip_core_usage entity.
Reference to the specific IP core being integrated. Identifies the IP block (e.g., PCIe controller, DDR PHY, USB subsystem) from the IP catalog.
Reference to the IC design project (SoC, ASIC, or FPGA design) into which this IP core version is being integrated. Links the usage record to the parent design context.

business_glossary_term: Source Design Project ID (IP Reuse Origin)
description: Reference to the original design project from which this IP core integration was reused, when ip_reuse_flag is True. Enables IP reuse lineage tracking and qualification inheritance assessment.
Reference to the tapeout event (final GDSII submission for manufacturing) in which this IP core integration was included. Links IP usage to the specific fabrication submission for traceability.
Physical silicon area consumed by this IP core integration in square micrometers (µm²). Used for die area budget tracking, PPA (Power, Performance, Area) analysis, and cost estimation.
Target operating clock frequency in megahertz (MHz) for this IP core integration in the design context. Used for timing closure planning and STA (Static Timing Analysis) constraint setup.

Serialized key-value string of configuration parameters applied to this IP core instance (e.g., data_width=64, num_channels=8, fifo_depth=512). Captures the specific parameterization used in this design context for reproducibility and IP reuse analytics.
Timestamp when this IP core usage record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail, data lineage, and record provenance tracking in the Databricks Silver Layer.

Full hierarchical path of the IP core instance within the design netlist (e.g., 'top/subsystem_a/pcie_ctrl_0'). Enables precise identification of the IP instance location in the design hierarchy for debug, ECO, and physical implementation.

Export control classification of this IP core under US EAR (Export Administration Regulations) or ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations). Determines permissible countries and end-users for design sharing and IP transfer. Critical for regulatory compliance.
Functional verification coverage percentage achieved for this IP core integration, as reported by the verification environment. Used for sign-off readiness assessment and quality gate enforcement.
Number of times this IP core is instantiated within the design project (e.g., 4 instances of a SERDES PHY). Drives royalty calculation for royalty-bearing IP and area budget planning.
Calendar date on which integration of this IP core into the design project was initiated. Used for project timeline tracking, TTM (Time to Market) analysis, and design schedule management.

Current lifecycle state of the IP core integration within the design project. Tracks progression from initial selection through silicon validation: planned (selected but not yet integrated), in_progress (integration underway), verified (simulation/emulation verified), silicon_proven (validated on fabricated silicon).

Primary bus or interface protocol used to connect this IP core to the SoC interconnect fabric (e.g., AXI4, AHB, APB, CHI, TileLink, OCP). Critical for integration verification and bus protocol compliance checking. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: AXI4|AHB|APB|CHI|TileLink|OCP|AXI4-Lite|AXI4-Stream|Wishbone|AMBA — promote to reference product]

Specific version identifier of the IP core being integrated (e.g., 2.1.0, 3.0.0-rc1). Tracks which release of the IP block is used in this design context to support IP lifecycle and change management.

Indicates whether this IP core integration is a reuse of a previously silicon-proven instance from another design project (True = reused IP, False = first-time integration). Enables IP reuse analytics and NRE cost avoidance tracking.

Indicates whether this IP core integration has passed DFM (Design for Manufacturability) rule checks for the target process node (True = DFM compliant). Critical for yield optimization and tapeout readiness sign-off.

Indicates whether DFT (Design for Testability) structures such as scan chains, BIST, or JTAG boundary scan are enabled for this IP core integration (True = DFT enabled). Impacts ATPG test pattern generation and ATE test coverage.

Indicates whether this IP core version has been validated on fabricated silicon within this specific design project context (True = silicon-proven, False = not yet silicon-proven). Key qualifier for IP reuse qualification and KGD (Known Good Die) assessments.
Timestamp when this IP core usage record was most recently modified. Used for change tracking, incremental data pipeline processing, and audit compliance.

Licensing model under which the IP core is used in this design: royalty_bearing (per-unit royalty owed on production), perpetual (one-time NRE fee, unlimited use), subscription (time-limited access), internal (internally developed IP, no external license), open_source (open-source licensed IP). Critical for license compliance auditing and cost accounting.

Free-text field for capturing integration-specific observations, known issues, workarounds, or deviations from standard integration procedures. Supports design review documentation and knowledge transfer.

Non-Recurring Engineering cost in US dollars associated with licensing or customizing this IP core for the design project. Includes one-time integration fees, customization charges, and support costs. Used for design project cost accounting.

Semiconductor process technology node for which this IP core version is qualified (e.g., '5nm', '7nm', '28nm'). Ensures IP-PDK compatibility and is critical for physical design sign-off and DFM compliance.
Estimated power consumption contributed by this IP core integration in milliwatts (mW), covering dynamic and static power. Used for top-level power budget analysis and PPA optimization.

business_glossary_term: IP Core Royalty Rate Per Unit (USD)
description: Per-unit royalty rate in US dollars payable to the IP licensor for each chip shipped containing this IP core. Applicable only for royalty_bearing license types. Used for royalty obligation forecasting and financial accruals.
Calendar date on which the formal design sign-off for this IP core integration was granted or last updated. Used for tapeout readiness tracking and audit trail.

Design sign-off status for this IP core integration, reflecting the outcome of formal design review and approval gates (pending, approved, rejected, waived). Required for tapeout readiness and quality gate compliance.
Calendar date on which silicon validation of this IP core integration was completed, confirming silicon-proven status. Null if silicon validation has not yet occurred.
Operating supply voltage in volts (V) for this IP core integration. Used for power domain planning, multi-voltage design verification, and UPF (Unified Power Format) constraint generation.

Timing budget allocated to this IP core integration in nanoseconds (ns), representing its contribution to the top-level timing closure budget. Derived from Synopsys PrimeTime timing analysis and used for STA sign-off.
FK to design.ip_core.ip_core_id — IP core usage records which IP core is being integrated. This is the fundamental FK for the association record.
Calendar date on which functional verification of this IP core integration was completed and signed off. Marks the transition from in_progress to verified integration status.

Verification methodology applied to validate this IP core integration (e.g., UVM, OVM, formal verification, emulation, FPGA prototype). Determines verification coverage and sign-off criteria. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: UVM|OVM|VMM|formal|emulation|FPGA_prototype|mixed — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Unique surrogate identifier for the PDK master record in the Databricks Silver Layer. Primary key for all downstream joins and lineage tracking.
Foreign key linking to product.process_node. Business justification: Ensures alignment of PDKs with official process‑node definitions for compliance, cost, and yield analysis in engineering reports.

Foreign key linking to supply.supplier. Business justification: PDK procurement and NDA management: design teams must track which supplier delivers each PDK version for license compliance, NDA enforcement, and supplier qualification audits. foundry_name is a denor
Part number assigned to this PDK in Oracle Agile PLM for product data and change management. Used to track PDK-related Engineering Change Orders (ECOs) and Product Change Notifications (PCNs).

SHA-256 cryptographic hash of the PDK distribution archive. Used to verify PDK file integrity after download or transfer, ensuring no corruption or unauthorized modification of foundry-provided design rule files.

Externally-known alphanumeric code uniquely identifying this PDK release, as assigned by the foundry or internal PDK management team (e.g., TSMC_N5_PDK_2023Q4). Used as the business-facing identifier in Cadence Virtuoso and Synopsys Design Compiler tool configurations.
Date on which this PDK version was deprecated and superseded by a newer release. Designs in progress must migrate to the active version by this date. Null if the PDK is still active.

Free-text description of the PDK, including key features, intended use cases, known limitations, and any special design guidelines. Provides context for design engineers selecting the appropriate PDK for a new IC design project.

business_glossary_term: Design for Testability (DFT) Rule Deck Version
description: Version of the Design for Testability (DFT) rule deck included in this PDK. DFT rules govern scan chain insertion, ATPG (Automatic Test Pattern Generation) compatibility, and boundary scan requirements for post-fabrication testing.

business_glossary_term: Design Rule Check (DRC) Rule Deck Version
description: Version identifier of the Design Rule Check (DRC) rule deck included in this PDK. The DRC rule deck enforces foundry-mandated physical design rules to ensure manufacturability. Used in Cadence Innovus and KLA ICOS inspection correlation.

business_glossary_term: Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tool Compatibility Matrix
description: Comma-separated list or reference identifier describing the EDA tool versions certified for use with this PDK (e.g., 'Cadence Virtuoso 23.1, Synopsys IC Compiler II 2023.03, Mentor Calibre 2023.1'). Ensures design teams use validated tool-PDK combinations for tapeout sign-off.

Export control classification of this PDK under US Export Administration Regulations (EAR) or ITAR. Determines which countries and entities may receive this PDK. Critical for compliance with BIS/EAR and CHIPS Act requirements.

Canonical file system or object storage path to the PDK installation directory or archive (e.g., '/pdk/tsmc/n5/v1.3.2' or 's3://pdk-repo/tsmc/n5/v1.3.2'). Used by EDA tool setup scripts to locate PDK assets in Cadence Virtuoso and Synopsys Design Compiler environments.

Layout data format supported by this PDK for tapeout submission to the foundry. GDSII (Graphic Data System II) is the legacy industry standard; OASIS is the modern compressed alternative. Determines the final tapeout file format.
Name of the I/O pad cell library included in this PDK, providing ESD-protected interface cells for chip-to-package signal routing. Required for full-chip physical design and package co-design.
Version of the foundry-provided hard IP library (e.g., PLLs, SerDes, PHYs) bundled with this PDK. IP cores are pre-characterized and validated for the specific process node.

Identifier or reference name for the metal layer stack definition included in this PDK, specifying the number of metal layers, dielectric materials, and interconnect hierarchy (FEOL/MOL/BEOL). Governs routing resources available to physical design tools.

Type of licensing agreement under which this PDK is distributed to design teams. 'foundry_standard' = included with foundry engagement; 'nre_included' = bundled with NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) agreement; 'subscription' = annual license fee; 'evaluation' = time-limited trial.

Primary lithography technology used in this process node as reflected in the PDK design rules. EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet) is used for leading-edge nodes (≤7nm); DUV (Deep Ultraviolet) with multi-patterning for older nodes. Impacts mask cost and design rule complexity.

business_glossary_term: Layout Versus Schematic (LVS) Rule Deck Version
description: Version identifier of the Layout Versus Schematic (LVS) rule deck included in this PDK. LVS verifies that the physical layout matches the circuit schematic. Mandatory for tapeout sign-off in Cadence Virtuoso flows.
Maximum operating supply voltage (Vdd) in volts supported by this process node as defined in the PDK. Exceeding this value risks device reliability and oxide breakdown.

Indicates whether this PDK includes or is compatible with a foundry-provided memory compiler for generating SRAM, ROM, or register file macros. True = memory compiler available; False = memory macros must be sourced separately.

Total number of metal interconnect layers defined in the PDK layer stack. Determines routing density, signal integrity characteristics, and manufacturing cost. Typical values range from 6 to 20+ for advanced nodes.
Minimum operating supply voltage (Vdd) in volts supported by this process node as defined in the PDK. Used for low-power design optimization and multi-voltage domain planning.
Indicates whether this PDK version is qualified and approved for use in Multi-Project Wafer (MPW) shuttle runs, enabling cost-shared prototyping across multiple design projects on a single wafer.
Human-readable descriptive name of the PDK (e.g., 'TSMC 5nm N5 PDK v1.3'). Used in EDA tool selection menus, design review documentation, and PLM records in Siemens Teamcenter.

Indicates whether access to this PDK requires a signed Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) with the foundry. True = NDA mandatory before distribution; False = PDK is available under standard licensing terms. Governs access control in Siemens Teamcenter PLM.
Nominal (typical) operating supply voltage in volts for this process node as specified in the PDK. Used as the reference voltage for standard timing and power characterization.

business_glossary_term: Optical Proximity Correction (OPC) Rule Deck Version
description: Version of the Optical Proximity Correction (OPC) rule deck included in this PDK. OPC rules compensate for photolithographic distortions during mask writing, critical for sub-10nm nodes using EUV or DUV lithography.

Maximum junction operating temperature in degrees Celsius for which the PDK device models and timing libraries are characterized. Defines the hot corner for timing sign-off and reliability qualification.
Minimum junction operating temperature in degrees Celsius for which the PDK device models and timing libraries are characterized. Defines the cold corner for timing sign-off.

Current lifecycle state of the PDK version. 'draft' = under development; 'active' = qualified and approved for production design starts; 'deprecated' = superseded by newer version but still supported; 'retired' = no longer supported; 'restricted' = limited access pending NDA or export control review.

Comma-separated list of process corners (PVT corners) defined in this PDK for timing and power sign-off (e.g., 'TT, FF, SS, FS, SF'). Each corner represents a combination of process, voltage, and temperature variation for worst/best-case analysis in Synopsys PrimeTime.

Foundry-specific process family or platform name within a node (e.g., 'N5', 'N5P', 'N4', 'SF3E'). Distinguishes performance-optimized, power-optimized, or cost-optimized variants within the same technology node.

Date on which the PDK version was internally qualified and approved for production IC design starts at Semiconductors. Distinct from the foundry release date; reflects internal DRC/LVS and SPICE model validation completion.
Date on which this PDK version was officially released by the foundry and made available for design use. Used for design start eligibility checks and tapeout qualification timelines.

URL pointing to the official PDK release notes document, detailing changes, bug fixes, and known issues relative to the previous version. Essential for design teams evaluating PDK upgrade impact on in-progress designs.

Identifier or name of the SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis) device model set bundled with this PDK (e.g., 'BSIM-CMG v110', 'PSP v103'). Used by Synopsys PrimeTime and Cadence Virtuoso for circuit simulation and timing analysis.
Version identifier of the SPICE model set included in this PDK. Critical for simulation reproducibility and sign-off correlation between design teams and the foundry.

Name of the primary standard cell library bundled with this PDK (e.g., 'SC9MC_HD', 'SC7P5T_HD'). Standard cell libraries provide pre-characterized logic gates used in RTL-to-GDS synthesis flows in Synopsys Design Compiler.
Version of the standard cell library included in this PDK release. Version changes may affect timing, power, and area (PPA) characteristics of synthesized designs.
Item identifier assigned to this PDK record in Siemens Teamcenter PLM, enabling traceability between the PDK master record and associated design projects, change orders, and release workflows.

Transistor architecture type used in this process node (e.g., FinFET, Gate-All-Around (GAA), Planar, FDSOI). Determines device modeling approach, SPICE model complexity, and applicable EDA tool flows.

Version string of the PDK release following semantic versioning conventions (e.g., '1.3.2', '2.0.0-beta'). Critical for design reproducibility, tapeout qualification, and EDA tool compatibility validation. Managed in Oracle Agile PLM and Siemens Teamcenter.
System-generated unique identifier for each calibration record.
Unique identifier of the fab equipment that was calibrated.
FK to equipment.fab_tool.fab_tool_id — Calibration records must reference the tool/instrument calibrated. Required for ISO 9001 traceability.

Foreign key linking to equipment.pm_schedule. Business justification: Calibration activities in semiconductor fabs are frequently scheduled as part of preventive maintenance programs — PM schedules often include calibration verification steps. Linking calibration_record
Planned interval in days between successive calibrations for this equipment.
Technique used to perform the calibration.
Current processing status of the calibration record.
Link to the detailed calibration report stored in the document repository.
Standardized code representing the calibration outcome for downstream analytics.
Name of the source system that generated the record (e.g., KLA ICOS, SmartFactory MES).
Reference standard used for calibration (e.g., NIST traceable, SEMI E30).
Date and time when the calibration activity was performed.
Method of calibration applied to the equipment.
Additional remarks or observations captured by the technician.
Reference to the compliance clause (e.g., ISO 9001, IATF 16949) satisfied by this calibration.
Timestamp when the calibration record was first created in the system.
Plant or fab line where the equipment resides (e.g., FAB1, LineA).
Identifier of the wafer lot associated with the calibration (if applicable).
Value recorded by the instrument during calibration.
Estimated uncertainty of the measured value.
Unit of the measured and nominal values (e.g., nanometers, volts).
Date by which the next calibration must be performed.
Target or nominal value that the equipment should meet.
Pass/Fail outcome of the calibration activity.
Full name of the calibration technician.
Timestamp of the most recent modification to the calibration record.
System-generated unique identifier for the maintenance event record.
FK to equipment.pm_schedule.pm_schedule_id — Scheduled PM events must link back to the PM schedule that triggered them, enabling PM compliance tracking.
FK to equipment.fab_tool.fab_tool_id — Every maintenance event must reference the tool serviced. Core operational FK for maintenance history and MTTR calculation.
FK to equipment.spare_part.spare_part_id — Maintenance events consume spare parts. This link enables parts consumption tracking and inventory depletion.
Identifier of the fab floor or site where the equipment resides.

Foreign key linking to quality.nonconformance_report. Business justification: In semiconductor fabs, tool excursions generating NCRs trigger maintenance events (tool hold and repair). Maintenance engineers need to reference the NCR that initiated the maintenance action for root
True if the maintenance altered the equipment baseline for qualification.
Additional free‑form notes captured by technicians or supervisors.
Regulatory framework applicable to the maintenance activity.
Three‑letter ISO currency code for monetary amounts.
Total equipment downtime measured in minutes.
Identifier of the ECO that drove the modification, if applicable.
Date‑time when the maintenance work was completed.
Human‑readable identifier or ticket number assigned to the maintenance event.
Classification of the maintenance activity.
Monetary cost of labor based on labor hours and rate.
Total technician labor time recorded for the event.
Strategic classification of the maintenance approach.
Current processing state of the maintenance event.
Estimated impact on Overall Equipment Effectiveness expressed as a percent.
Aggregate cost of all spare parts and consumables used.
Planned performance gain (e.g., throughput increase) resulting from the event.
Snapshot or version identifier of equipment configuration after maintenance.
Snapshot or version identifier of equipment configuration before maintenance.
Timestamp when the maintenance event record was first created in the system.
Timestamp of the most recent update to the maintenance event record.
Narrative of the corrective actions performed during the event.
Indicates whether a formal re‑qualification is needed after the event.
Current status of any required re‑qualification process.
High‑level classification of the underlying cause of failure.
Free‑text description of the specific root cause identified.
Details of any safety incident associated with the maintenance.
True if the maintenance event involved a safety incident.
Date‑time when the maintenance work actually began.
Full name of the technician responsible for the maintenance.
Combined cost of parts, labor, and any additional charges.
Origin that caused the maintenance event to be created.
System user identifier that last modified the record.
Indicates whether the event involved a hardware, software, or firmware upgrade.
System user identifier that created the record.
Unique surrogate key for each OEE measurement record.
Identifier of the wafer lot being processed during the measurement period.
FK to equipment.fab_tool.fab_tool_id — OEE records must reference the measured tool. This is the fundamental relationship for equipment productivity reporting.

Foreign key linking to equipment.maintenance_event. Business justification: OEE records track equipment state transitions and downtime categories. When a maintenance event causes a state change (e.g., tool goes from productive to unscheduled downtime), linking the oee_record

Foreign key linking to equipment.tool_chamber. Business justification: OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) metrics in semiconductor fabs are tracked at both the tool level and the chamber level for multi-chamber systems. A CVD cluster tool with 4 chambers may have diff

Foreign key linking to fabrication.fabrication_process_step. Business justification: Step-level OEE reporting is a standard semiconductor manufacturing KPI — OEE is measured per tool per process operation to identify bottleneck steps. Linking OEE records to specific process steps enab
Percentage of scheduled time the equipment was available for production.
Total scheduled time the equipment was available for production.
Free‑form notes or observations related to the OEE measurement.
Timestamp when the OEE record was first inserted into the data lake.
High‑level classification of downtime type.
Standard code describing the root cause of downtime.
Hours spent in engineering or setup state (non‑productive).
Exact date‑time when the OEE measurement was recorded.
Hours the equipment was idle (no work) while available.
Granularity of the OEE aggregation (e.g., shift, day, week).

Target thickness in nanometers for deposition or remaining thickness after etch/CMP operations.
Target uniformity percentage for thickness, composition, or other critical parameters across the wafer surface.
Waste elimination strategy (e.g., heat_recovery, coolant_reclaim, lean_idle_shutdown).
Whether waste-heat recovery is enabled.
Whether waste heat is recovered/reused.
Target yield percentage for wafers processed using this recipe, used for performance monitoring and continuous improvement.
Unique identifier for the process step within the FAB routing. Primary key for the process step entity.

Reference to the parent process flow routing that contains this step. Links the step to its overall manufacturing route.
Reference to the target process recipe that defines the detailed parameters, settings, and control logic for executing this step on the equipment.
Reference to the target process step to which lots are routed when rework is triggered from this step. Null if no rework loop exists.
Date when the process step was approved for production use. Used for change control and audit trails.
Approval workflow status for the process step definition. Tracks engineering change control and qualification approval.
Name or identifier of the engineer or manager who approved this process step for production use. Used for traceability and accountability.
Number of wafers or lots that can be processed together in a single batch at this step. Null for single-wafer processing steps.
Engineering change order or change control number associated with the creation or modification of this process step. Links to formal change management process.
Coolant flow rate in liters per minute.
Whether coolant is recirculated to reduce waste.
Percentage of coolant reclaimed/recycled.
Volume of coolant waste generated.
Reference code to the applicable cooling condition specification.
Duration of the cooling stage.
Energy consumed by the cooling process.
Cooling medium used (e.g., DI water, helium backside, chilled nitrogen).
Cooling method applied (e.g., convective, conductive, cryogenic, water-cooled chuck).
Whether cooling optimization is enabled for this step/recipe.
Cooling optimization option selected (e.g., energy_min, throughput_max, balanced).
Indicates whether this step/recipe includes a cooling process stage.
Controlled cooling ramp-down rate.
Indicates whether a cooling step is required after this process step.
Target wafer/chamber temperature after cooling.
Financial cost center to which this process step is allocated for cost accounting and budgeting purposes.
Timestamp when this process step record was first created in the system. Used for audit trails and data lineage.
Boolean flag indicating whether this step is critical to product quality or yield. Critical steps require enhanced monitoring, control, and approval workflows.
Date when this process step definition is retired or superseded. Null for currently active steps. Supports historical tracking and version control.
Date when this process step definition becomes effective and can be used in production routing. Supports time-based version control.
Constraint specifying the class or type of equipment required to execute this process step. Used by MES for equipment qualification and dispatching.
Boolean flag indicating whether inline inspection or metrology is required after this step. True if inspection is mandatory before proceeding to the next step.
User identifier of the person who last modified this process step record. Used for accountability and audit trails.
Timestamp when this process step record was last modified. Used for change tracking and audit trails.
Maximum allowable queue time in minutes before the step must be processed or the lot is flagged for hold. Critical for time-sensitive processes.
Minimum number of wafers or lots required to start processing at this step. Used for batch tool optimization and scheduling.

business_glossary_term: Operation Type (Front End of Line / Middle of Line / Back End of Line)
description: High-level classification of the process step into Front End of Line (FEOL), Middle of Line (MOL), or Back End of Line (BEOL) manufacturing phases.

Detailed classification of the process step by operation category such as lithography, etch, deposition (CVD/PVD/ALD), Chemical Mechanical Planarization (CMP), ion implant, thermal anneal, clean, wet clean, inspection, or metrology. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: lithography|etch|deposition|CMP|implant|anneal|clean|wet_clean|inspection|metrology — 10 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Granular subcategory within the process category, such as EUV lithography, DUV lithography, plasma etch, reactive ion etch, CVD, PVD, ALD, etc. Provides detailed process technology classification.
Boolean flag indicating whether this step is part of a rework loop. True if the step can route back to an earlier step for defect correction or reprocessing.
Percentage of lots or wafers that must be sampled for inspection or metrology at this step. Used for statistical quality control.
Boolean flag indicating whether this step can be skipped under certain conditions (e.g., engineering wafers, test lots). True if skip is permitted.
Expected queue time in minutes before the step begins processing. Used for scheduling and WIP prediction.
Standardized alphanumeric code uniquely identifying the step type across all FAB operations. Used for cross-facility consistency and reporting.
Standard cost per wafer for executing this process step. Used for product costing, yield analysis, and financial planning. Business-confidential financial data.
Detailed textual description of the process step, including purpose, key parameters, and special handling instructions for operators and engineers.
Human-readable name or identifier for the process step. Used for operator reference and MES display.
Sequential ordering of this step within the process flow routing. Determines the execution order of operations in the FAB.

Current lifecycle status of the process step definition. Active steps are in production use; inactive or obsolete steps are retired; engineering steps are under development; pending approval steps await qualification.
Step-level target cycle time in minutes. Used for capacity planning, scheduling, and WIP flow optimization.
Version identifier for the process step definition. Incremented with each engineering change. Supports change control and traceability.
Waste elimination strategy (e.g., heat_recovery, coolant_reclaim, lean_idle_shutdown).
Whether waste heat is recovered/reused.
Unique identifier for the semiconductor technology node record. Primary key.
Identifier of the primary fabrication facility where this technology node is manufactured. Links to the fab facility master data.

Foreign key linking to product.process_node. Business justification: product.fabrication_technology_node and product.process_node represent the same real-world technology node from fab and product domain perspectives. Linking them enables cross-domain node reconcil
Indicates whether this technology node record is currently active and available for use in manufacturing and design operations. False indicates archived or deprecated nodes.
Timestamp when this technology node record was first created in the system. Audit trail for data lineage and compliance.
Complexity level of Design for Manufacturability (DFM) rules required for this node. Higher complexity nodes require more sophisticated Optical Proximity Correction (OPC) and restricted design rules.

Indicates whether Design for Manufacturability (DFM) checks and optimizations are mandatory for designs targeting this node. True for advanced nodes requiring OPC, dummy fill, and restricted design rules.
Indicates whether Design for Testability (DFT) structures (scan chains, BIST, ATPG patterns) are required for designs on this node. Ensures adequate test coverage for quality assurance.
Export Control Classification Number (ECCN) under US Export Administration Regulations (EAR) for this technology node. Determines export licensing requirements to restricted countries.
Date when the technology node was officially announced for end-of-life phase-out. Triggers Product Change Notification (PCN) and Last Time Buy (LTB) processes for customers.

business_glossary_term: International Automotive Task Force (IATF) 16949 Certified Flag
description: Indicates whether this technology node is certified under IATF 16949 Automotive Quality Management standards, required for automotive-grade semiconductor production.
Indicates whether the manufacturing process for this technology node is certified under ISO 9001 Quality Management System standards.

business_glossary_term: International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) Controlled Flag
description: Indicates whether this technology node is subject to ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations) export controls due to defense or aerospace applications. Restricts international technology transfer.
Timestamp when this technology node record was last updated. Tracks data currency and change history for audit and compliance purposes.
Primary lithography technology used for patterning in this node. DUV (Deep Ultraviolet) for mature nodes, EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet) for advanced nodes below 7nm.
Cost of a full photomask set for this technology node in US dollars. Significant cost driver for advanced nodes using EUV lithography. Business-confidential pricing data.
Total number of metal interconnect layers available in the Back End of Line (BEOL) stack for this technology node. Typically ranges from 6 to 15+ layers depending on node complexity.
The smallest feature dimension that can be reliably manufactured in this technology node, measured in nanometers. Critical dimension for lithography and patterning capability.
Identifier of the user or system process that last modified this technology node record. Supports audit trail and accountability requirements.
Internal alphanumeric code used to uniquely identify the technology node in manufacturing execution systems and product lifecycle management systems.
Generation classification of the node (e.g., Leading Edge, Mature, Legacy). Indicates the relative maturity and market positioning of the process technology.
Human-readable name of the technology node (e.g., 5nm, 7nm, 14nm, 28nm, 65nm, 90nm). Industry-standard designation for the process generation.

Estimated Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) cost in US dollars for a typical design tapeout on this technology node. Includes mask set costs, design services, and qualification expenses. Business-confidential pricing data.
Qualitative or quantitative assessment of the node's Power, Performance, and Area (PPA) characteristics relative to prior generations. Critical metric for competitive positioning.
Version identifier of the manufacturing process flow (sequence of FEOL, MOL, BEOL steps) defined for this technology node. Tracks process recipe evolution and engineering changes.
Date when the technology node was certified as production-ready and available for high-volume manufacturing (HVM). Null if still in development or qualification.

Current lifecycle status of the technology node. Development: R&D phase. Qualification: undergoing reliability and yield validation. Qualified: approved for production. Production: active manufacturing. Mature: stable high-volume. EOL (End of Life): phase-out planned.

business_glossary_term: Registration Evaluation Authorization and Restriction of Chemicals (REACH) Compliant Flag
description: Indicates whether the chemicals and materials used in this technology node comply with EU REACH Regulation for chemical safety and substance registration.

business_glossary_term: Restriction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS) Compliant Flag
description: Indicates whether the manufacturing process for this technology node complies with EU RoHS Directive restrictions on hazardous substances (lead, mercury, cadmium, etc.).
Comma-separated list of target application domains optimized for this node (e.g., Mobile, Automotive, AI/ML, IoT, High-Performance Computing, Networking). Guides product design-in decisions.
Target percentage of good dies per wafer for this technology node at maturity. Key performance indicator for manufacturing efficiency and cost competitiveness.
Type of transistor structure used in this technology node. Planar for older nodes, FinFET (Fin Field-Effect Transistor) for 22nm-7nm, GAA (Gate All Around) for 3nm and below.
Standard wafer diameter supported by this technology node, typically 200mm (8-inch) or 300mm (12-inch). Determines fab equipment compatibility and die yield economics.
Unique identifier for the wafer lot tracked through the FAB from wafer start to lot disposition. Primary key for all WIP lot tracking across FEOL, MOL, and BEOL operations.
Foreign key linking to customer.account. Business justification: Required for lot‑level billing and customer account reporting; each wafer lot is charged to a specific customer account.

Foreign key linking to sales.booking. Business justification: Fab planners prioritize and track wafer lots against confirmed customer bookings for on-time delivery reporting and revenue recognition. A booking is the confirmed demand event that a hot lot or prior

Foreign key linking to quality.control_plan. Business justification: A wafer lot is processed under a specific control plan version active at production time. IATF 16949 and customer quality agreements require lot-to-control-plan traceability for quality audits and cus

Foreign key linking to sales.customer_contract. Business justification: Wafer lots manufactured under long-term supply agreements (LTSAs) must reference the governing customer contract to validate volume commitments, technology node compliance, and PCN obligations. Fab op

Foreign key linking to fabrication.fab_facility. Business justification: A wafer lot is processed at a specific fab facility. fabrication_wafer_lot currently stores fab_facility_code as a denormalized string with no FK to fab_facility. Adding fab_facility_id normalizes thi
Foreign key linking to product.ic_catalog. Business justification: Required for Production Planning Report linking each wafer lot to the specific IC catalog item being manufactured.

Foreign key linking to design.ic_design_project. Business justification: Required for lot‑level cost and yield reporting per design project, enabling profitability analysis and project KPI dashboards.
Reference to the parent lot from which this lot was split or derived. Null for original lots. Enables wafer genealogy tracking and traceability.
Unique identifier for the process route (recipe sequence) assigned to this lot. Defines the complete sequence of operations from wafer start to completion.

Foreign key linking to product.sku. Business justification: Production planning and order fulfillment track wafer lots against specific SKUs (speed grade, voltage, temperature bin) to calculate commit quantities and manage process splits. Semiconductor fabs re

Foreign key linking to fabrication.technology_node. Business justification: Wafer lot references a technology node; replace string column with FK to technology_node for normalization.
Date and time when the lot completed all FAB processing operations and was dispositioned. Null for lots still in WIP.
Descriptive name of the current process operation (e.g., CVD_OXIDE_DEP, EUV_LITHO, CMP_POLISH). Provides human-readable context for lot location.
Sequential operation step number in the process route where the lot is currently located. Used for tracking lot position in the manufacturing flow.

High-level FAB area classification where the lot is currently located: FEOL (front-end-of-line transistor formation), MOL (middle-of-line contacts), BEOL (back-end-of-line interconnect), metrology (inspection), or test (electrical probe).
Total elapsed time in days from wafer start to lot completion. Key performance metric for manufacturing efficiency and customer responsiveness.
Target completion date for the lot to meet customer delivery commitments. Used for scheduling and on-time-delivery tracking.
Indicates whether the lot is currently on hold and prevented from processing. True when lot is held for quality, engineering, or disposition review.
Standardized code indicating the reason for lot hold (e.g., QUAL_FAIL, ENG_REVIEW, EQUIP_DOWN, MATL_ISSUE). Null when hold_flag is false.
Date and time when the lot was placed on hold. Used to calculate hold duration and impact on cycle time. Null when lot is not on hold.
Original number of wafers when the lot was started. Used to calculate yield loss and scrap rate during processing.
Indicates whether this lot is designated as a hot lot requiring expedited processing and priority resource allocation. True for urgent customer orders.
Date and time when the lot record was first created in the MES system. May precede wafer start for planning purposes.

Final disposition decision for the completed lot: pass (released to next stage), fail (rejected), partial (some wafers pass), rework (reprocess), scrap (discard), or engineering_hold (pending review).
Free-text field for operators and engineers to record important observations, special handling instructions, or process deviations for this lot.
Externally-known unique business identifier for the wafer lot assigned by MES at wafer start. Used for tracking and traceability across all FAB operations and external communications.
FK to fabrication.technology_node.technology_node_id — Every lot is manufactured on a specific technology node. This is fundamental for WIP classification and routing.

Classification of the wafer lot purpose: production (revenue-generating), engineering (process development), qualification (product validation), MPW (multi-project wafer shuttle), pilot (pre-production), or rework (reprocessed lot).
Date and time when the lot record was last modified in the MES system. Tracks most recent status or attribute change.
Identifies the source MES system that created and manages this lot record: Camstar MES, Applied Materials SmartFactory MES, or other. Used for data lineage and system integration.
Forecasted completion date based on current WIP position, remaining operations, and standard cycle times. Updated dynamically as lot progresses.
Scheduling priority assigned to the lot for FAB resource allocation. Hot and expedite lots receive preferential processing to meet urgent customer commitments.
Technology node of the semiconductor process measured in nanometers (e.g., 7nm, 5nm, 3nm). Defines the minimum feature size and process complexity.
Cumulative time in hours the lot has spent in active processing on equipment. Excludes queue and hold time. Used for capacity planning.
Human-readable name of the IC product being manufactured. Used for reporting and customer communications.
Cumulative time in hours the lot has spent waiting in queue between operations. Excludes active processing time. Used to identify bottlenecks.
Number of times the lot has been reworked or reprocessed through specific operations. Tracks process stability and quality issues.
Version number of the process route. Tracks recipe changes and process improvements over time for traceability and yield analysis.
Code identifying the quality sampling and inspection plan applied to this lot. Defines which wafers are sampled and at which operations for metrology and defect inspection.
Total number of wafers scrapped during processing due to defects, breakage, or sampling. Used to calculate yield loss and process capability.
Sequential number assigned when a lot is split into multiple child lots. Used with parent_lot_id to track lot genealogy and wafer provenance.
Current number of wafers in the lot. May decrease due to scraps, breakage, or sampling during processing. Initial count typically 25 wafers per lot for standard production.
Diameter of the silicon wafers in the lot measured in millimeters. Standard sizes include 200mm (8-inch) and 300mm (12-inch).
Date and time when the wafer lot was officially started in the FAB and entered WIP tracking. Represents the beginning of the lot's manufacturing lifecycle.
Current lifecycle status of the wafer lot in the FAB workflow. Tracks lot progression from queue through processing to final disposition.
Unique identifier for the fabrication lot hold event record.
Reference to the customer for whom the lot is being fabricated, relevant for customer-initiated holds or customer notification.

Reference to the fabrication facility where the hold event occurred.
Reference to the equipment unit associated with the hold event, if applicable (e.g., tool that triggered the excursion).

Foreign key linking to quality.nonconformance_report. Business justification: Lot holds are initiated by or linked to NCRs documenting the quality event causing the hold. fabrication_lot_hold.ncr_number is a denormalized reference to nonconformance_report.ncr_number. Replacing
Reference to the wafer lot that is placed on hold.
Reference to the specific process step (FEOL, MOL, BEOL operation) where the lot was held.
Reference to the product (IC design, SoC, ASIC) being fabricated in the lot under hold.

Foreign key linking to equipment.tool_chamber. Business justification: Chamber-level lot holds are a real fab operation — when a specific chamber fails qualification or shows contamination, lots processed in that chamber are placed on hold. This chamber-to-hold traceabil

Foreign key linking to test.bin_definition. Business justification: Lot holds triggered by bin yield failures reference the specific bin definition that caused the hold. Linking enables yield-based hold disposition rules, bin-specific corrective actions, and quality r

Foreign key linking to test.parametric_measurement. Business justification: fabrication_lot_hold has spc_rule_violation flag indicating holds triggered by parametric SPC excursions. Linking to the specific parametric measurement that triggered the hold is required for SPC-dri

description: Foreign key linking to test.wafer_probe_run. Business justification: Lot holds are frequently triggered by wafer probe yield failures. Linking the hold to the triggering probe run enables root cause analysis, hold disposition workflows, and regulatory traceability — a
Indicator whether management or engineering approval is required before the hold can be released.
Date and time when the hold release was approved.
Timestamp when this hold record was first created in the data system.
Indicator whether the customer must be notified of this hold event per contractual or quality agreement terms.
Date and time when the customer was notified of the hold event.
Indicator whether the hold was triggered by defect density exceeding acceptable limits.
Action taken upon hold release or closure (resume normal processing, rework, scrap, quarantine, return to customer, engineering review).
Free-text notes documenting the disposition decision, corrective actions taken, or investigation findings.
Indicator whether the hold has been escalated to senior engineering or management for resolution.
Duration in hours between hold placement and release, used for cycle time tracking and excursion management KPIs.
Date and time when the hold automatically expires if not explicitly released or extended, used for time-bound holds.
Date and time when the hold was placed on the lot, representing the principal business event timestamp.
Standardized code indicating the reason for placing the lot on hold (e.g., process excursion, quality issue, engineering investigation, customer request).
Detailed textual description of the reason for the hold, providing additional context beyond the reason code.
Date and time when the hold was released and the lot was cleared to resume processing. Null if hold is still active.

Timestamp when this hold record was last updated in the data system.

Unique transaction identifier from the MES system that recorded this hold event.

Standardized code identifying the root cause determined during investigation (e.g., equipment malfunction, operator error, material defect).
Specific SPC rule that was violated and triggered the hold (e.g., Western Electric Rule 1, 2, 3, 4).
Number of wafers in the lot at the time the hold was placed.
Unique identifier for the lot move transaction. Primary key for this WIP (Work in Process) lot movement event through a FAB (Fabrication Facility) process step.

Identifier for the FAB (Fabrication Facility) equipment or tool used to process this lot move (e.g., ATE (Automatic Test Equipment), lithography stepper, etcher, deposition chamber). Links to equipment master data.

Identifier for the IC (Integrated Circuit) product or device being manufactured in this lot. Links to product master data (e.g., SoC (System on Chip), ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit), FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)).

Foreign key linking to quality.inspection_lot. Business justification: In MES-driven semiconductor fabs, lot moves at inspection steps directly trigger or reference an inspection lot. This is a core MES-to-QMS integration point — the lot_move records the physical step ex

Foreign key linking to supply.material_master. Business justification: Lot moves record actual material consumption per process execution. Linking to material_master enables actual-vs-planned material consumption reporting, supplier quality correlation with yield excursi
Unique identifier for the WIP (Work in Process) lot being moved. References the wafer lot entity tracked through the FAB (Fabrication Facility).

Foreign key linking to fabrication.fabrication_process_step. Business justification: A lot move transaction records the specific process step at which the lot is being moved (move-in/move-out). lot_move currently has at_step as a BIGINT that semantically represents the process step bu

Identifier for the specific process chamber or module within the equipment used for this lot move. Relevant for multi-chamber tools (e.g., CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition), PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition), ALD (Atomic Layer Deposition) systems).

Identifier for the manufacturing work order or production order associated with this lot move. Links to ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) or MES (Manufacturing Execution System) work order master data.
Actual gas flow rate in SCCM (Standard Cubic Centimeters per Minute) for process gases during this lot move. Critical parameter for deposition and etch processes.

Actual RF (Radio Frequency) or DC power in watts applied during this lot move. Critical parameter for plasma processes (etch, PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition), PECVD (Plasma-Enhanced Chemical Vapor Deposition)).
Actual process chamber pressure in Torr recorded during this lot move. Critical parameter for vacuum processes (PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition), CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition), etch, implant).
Actual process temperature in degrees Celsius recorded during this lot move. Critical parameter for thermal processes (CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition), diffusion, anneal, oxidation).
Free-text comments or notes entered by the operator or engineer regarding this lot move. Captures contextual information, issues, or special handling instructions.
Identifier for the MES (Manufacturing Execution System) control job that orchestrated this lot move. Links to the higher-level job or batch execution context in Camstar MES or SmartFactory MES.
Coolant flow rate observed.
Coolant reclaim percentage.
Coolant waste volume generated.
Duration of cooling observed.
Energy consumed by cooling.
Cooling method applied.
Whether cooling optimization was active.
Cooling optimization option in effect.
Indicates whether a cooling process is associated.
Measured cooling ramp-down rate.
Timestamp when this lot move record was first created in the source system. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used for data lineage and audit trail.
Number of defects detected during or after this lot move, as reported by inline inspection or metrology systems. Used for yield analysis and SPC (Statistical Process Control).
Quality disposition or pass/fail outcome of the lot move. Determines whether the lot proceeds to the next step, requires rework, is scrapped, or is placed on hold for further inspection.
Code indicating the reason for a lot hold, if applicable (e.g., quality issue, equipment failure, engineering review, customer request). Empty if lot is not on hold.
Timestamp when this lot move record was last updated in the source system. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used for change tracking and data synchronization.
Measured temperature during/after cooling.
Unit of measure for the measurement_value field (e.g., nm (nanometers), um (micrometers), angstrom, ohm-cm (resistivity), percent, ppm (parts per million)).
Primary metrology measurement value captured during this lot move (e.g., film thickness, CD (Critical Dimension), overlay, resistivity). Unit of measure is context-dependent on the operation type.
Timestamp when the lot was moved into the process step or equipment. Marks the start of processing for this operation. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.
Timestamp when the lot was moved out of the process step or equipment. Marks the completion of processing for this operation. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.
Current lifecycle status of the lot move transaction. Indicates whether the move completed successfully, is still in progress, was aborted, held for review, or skipped.
Scheduling priority level for this lot move. Hot and expedite lots receive preferential processing to meet urgent customer commitments or TTM (Time to Market) targets.

Manufacturing layer classification for this process step. FEOL (Front End of Line) covers transistor formation, MOL (Middle of Line) covers contacts, BEOL (Back End of Line) covers interconnects and metallization.

High-level process module category for this operation (e.g., lithography, etch, deposition, ion implantation, CMP (Chemical Mechanical Planarization), diffusion, metrology, inspection, test). Actual processing time in seconds for this lot move, calculated as the duration between move-in and move-out timestamps. Used for cycle time analysis and equipment utilization tracking.
Number of wafers or units in the lot at move-in. Represents the starting quantity before processing.
Number of wafers or units in the lot at move-out. Represents the ending quantity after processing. May differ from quantity_in due to scrap, breakage, or sampling.
Time in seconds the lot spent waiting in queue before move-in. Measures WIP (Work in Process) wait time and identifies bottlenecks in the FAB (Fabrication Facility) flow.

Number of wafers or units scrapped during this lot move due to breakage, defects, or process failures. Used for yield loss tracking and DPPM (Defective Parts Per Million) calculation.
Process technology node for this lot (e.g., 5nm, 7nm, 14nm, 28nm, 180nm). Defines the minimum feature size and process generation.

Waste elimination strategy applied.
Whether waste heat was recovered.
Unique identifier for the individual silicon wafer within the fabrication facility. Primary key for wafer tracking across all FAB (Fabrication Facility) process steps.
Reference to the fabrication facility where this wafer is being processed. Supports multi-site operations and capacity planning.
Reference to the parent wafer lot that this wafer belongs to. Wafers are processed in lots through the FAB (Fabrication Facility).
Reference to the IC (Integrated Circuit) design project that this wafer is fabricating. Links to design specifications, GDS (Graphic Data System) files, and tapeout records.

Timestamp when the wafer completed all FAB (Fabrication Facility) process steps and is ready for wafer probing or shipment to OSAT (Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test). Null for in-process wafers.
Number of critical defects that are likely to cause die failure. Subset of total defect count, used for yield prediction and SPC (Statistical Process Control).
Crystallographic orientation of the silicon wafer surface, specified using Miller indices. <100> is most common for CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) fabrication.
Sequential operation number in the process route that the wafer is currently at. Used for tracking progress through the hundreds of steps in IC (Integrated Circuit) fabrication.

Name or code of the current fabrication process step the wafer is at or has completed. Examples: lithography, CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition), CMP (Chemical Mechanical Planarization), ion implantation.
Total number of defects detected on the wafer surface through inspection systems. Tracked across process steps to monitor yield and process control.
Diameter of the wafer in millimeters. Standard sizes include 150mm (6 inch), 200mm (8 inch), and 300mm (12 inch). Larger diameters enable more dies per wafer.
Current disposition status of the wafer in the FAB (Fabrication Facility) workflow. Tracks lifecycle state from WIP (Work in Process) through completion or scrap.
Electrical doping type of the substrate. P-type (boron-doped) or N-type (phosphorus/arsenic-doped) determines the majority carrier type. Intrinsic wafers are undoped.
Indicates whether the wafer has an epitaxial layer grown on the substrate. Epitaxial wafers have a thin crystalline layer with controlled properties for advanced device fabrication.
Electrical resistivity of the epitaxial layer in ohm-centimeters, if present. Null for non-epitaxial wafers. Often differs from substrate resistivity.
Thickness of the epitaxial layer in micrometers, if present. Null for non-epitaxial wafers. Typical range: 1-20 micrometers.
Expected number of dies (individual chips) on this wafer based on die size and wafer diameter. Used for yield calculation and planning.
Hierarchical path tracking the wafer lineage from ingot to lot to wafer. Enables traceability for quality investigations and compliance. Format varies by MES (Manufacturing Execution System).
Actual number of dies that passed electrical testing and quality inspection. Populated after wafer probing and testing. Used to calculate wafer yield.
Code indicating the reason for wafer hold or quarantine, if applicable. Examples: out-of-spec measurement, equipment issue, quality investigation. Null for non-held wafers.
Timestamp of the most recent defect inspection or metrology measurement performed on this wafer. Null if no inspection has been performed yet.
Timestamp of the most recent process step or operation performed on this wafer. Used for WIP (Work in Process) tracking and cycle time analysis.
Angular position of the wafer notch in degrees, used for wafer alignment and orientation during automated processing. Standard is 0 degrees for <110> direction.
Processing priority level for this wafer. Critical and high priority wafers receive expedited processing to meet customer commitments or TTM (Time to Market) requirements.
Timestamp when this wafer record was first created in the data system. Audit field for data lineage and compliance.
Timestamp when this wafer record was last updated in the data system. Audit field for data lineage and change tracking.
Electrical resistivity of the wafer substrate in ohm-centimeters. Indicates doping concentration and electrical properties. Typical range: 0.001 to 100+ ohm-cm.
Number of times this wafer has been reworked or reprocessed due to process excursions or quality issues. Tracked for yield analysis and process improvement.
Code indicating the reason for wafer scrap, if applicable. Examples: excessive defects, process excursion, handling damage, contamination. Null for non-scrapped wafers.
Physical slot position of the wafer within the carrier or cassette during processing. Used for tracking and automation.
Timestamp when the wafer entered the FAB (Fabrication Facility) and began processing. Marks the beginning of the wafer lifecycle for cycle time tracking.

Material composition of the wafer substrate. Silicon is most common, but compound semiconductors like GaAs (Gallium Arsenide), SiC (Silicon Carbide), and GaN (Gallium Nitride) are used for specialized applications.
Thickness of the wafer in micrometers. Typical range is 500-800 micrometers depending on diameter and application. Critical for mechanical stability and process control.
Business identifier for the wafer within its lot. Typically a sequential number or alphanumeric code assigned during lot creation.
Unique identifier for the wafer start transaction. Primary key for the wafer start record.

Identifier for the photomask set (reticle set) used for lithography steps in this wafer lot. Links to the physical layout and GDS data.
Foreign key linking to product.ic_catalog. Business justification: Supports Wafer Start Scheduling report tying start orders to the IC catalog entry.

Foreign key linking to supply.material_master. Business justification: Needed for Wafer Start Material Certification process, tying each wafer start to the supplied material master record for quality and compliance tracking.
Identifier for the manufacturing process flow (recipe sequence) that this wafer lot will follow. Defines the FEOL, MOL, and BEOL steps.

Foreign key linking to product.product_spec. Business justification: Wafer starts are initiated against specific product specifications defining target electrical parameters, process corners, and yield requirements. NPI and production ramp workflows require traceabilit

Foreign key linking to inventory.raw_material. Business justification: Wafer starts consume specific raw silicon wafer inventory. Linking wafer_start to raw_material enables raw material consumption tracking, goods issue posting, and substrate traceability required for p

Foreign key linking to product.sku. Business justification: Wafer starts are authorized against specific SKU commitments — production schedulers release starts per SKU to ensure correct process splits for speed/voltage/temperature bins. SKU-level wafer start t

Foreign key linking to design.tapeout. Business justification: Wafer starts are directly authorized by an approved tapeout — the tapeout defines the mask set, GDS version, and NRE scope that governs production. Foundries track tapeout-to-wafer-start traceability

Foreign key linking to fabrication.fabrication_technology_node. Business justification: A wafer start is authorized for a specific technology node, which determines the process flow, mask set, and equipment requirements. wafer_start currently stores technology_node as a denormalized stri
Date and time when the wafer start was formally authorized in the MES system. Precedes the actual release to the FAB line.
System timestamp when this wafer start record was first created in the MES database. Used for audit trail and data lineage.
Crystallographic orientation of the silicon wafer, expressed in Miller indices (e.g., <100>, <111>). Affects electrical properties and process compatibility.
Electrical doping type of the starting wafer substrate: p-type (boron-doped), n-type (phosphorus or arsenic-doped), or intrinsic (undoped).
Export Control Classification Number (ECCN) under EAR for this product. Format: 5-character code (e.g., 3A001). Null if not export-controlled.
Planned total cycle time from wafer start to completion, measured in days. Based on standard process flow duration and current FAB loading.
Code indicating the reason for placing this wafer start on hold, if applicable. Examples: quality issue, material shortage, engineering review, customer request.

business_glossary_term: International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) Controlled Flag
description: Boolean indicator of whether this wafer lot contains ITAR-controlled technology requiring export compliance controls. True if ITAR applies; False otherwise.
System timestamp when this wafer start record was last updated. Tracks the most recent change to any field in the record.
Manufacturing lot identifier assigned to the wafer batch at start. This is the primary tracking identifier throughout FAB operations.
Reference to the NRE project if this wafer start is part of a customer-funded development program. Used for cost tracking and milestone billing.
Lot number of the parent lot if this wafer start is a split or rework from an existing lot. Null for original starts. Enables wafer genealogy tracking.
Target date for completing all FAB processing steps and releasing the lot to wafer test. Based on standard cycle time for the process flow.
Scheduling priority for this wafer lot in the FAB line. Hot lots receive expedited processing; engineering lots may have flexible timing; standard and low follow normal queue discipline.
Specific production line or bay within the FAB facility assigned to this wafer start. Determines equipment set and process capability.
Date and time when the wafer lot was physically released into the FAB line and began processing. Marks the start of cycle time measurement.
Customer-requested delivery date for finished wafers or packaged units. Drives priority and scheduling decisions.
Electrical resistivity of the starting wafer substrate, measured in ohm-centimeters. Indicates doping concentration and electrical characteristics.
Free-text field for any special handling instructions, process deviations, or notes that operators and engineers should be aware of for this wafer lot.
Free-text explanation of why this lot was split from a parent lot, if applicable. Examples: process experiment, capacity balancing, quality segregation.

Type of semiconductor substrate material used for this wafer lot. Silicon is standard; SOI (Silicon on Insulator), GaAs (Gallium Arsenide), GaN (Gallium Nitride), and SiC (Silicon Carbide) are specialty materials.
Number of wafers authorized and started in this lot. Represents the initial wafer count at FAB entry before any processing losses.
Diameter of the silicon wafer in millimeters. Standard values are 200mm or 300mm. Determines compatible equipment and process chambers.
Calendar date when the wafer lot was authorized and initiated into the FAB line. Principal business event timestamp for capacity planning and WIP tracking.
Business identifier for the wafer start transaction, externally visible and used for tracking and reporting. Format: WS followed by 10 digits.

Current lifecycle status of the wafer start transaction. Authorized: approved but not yet released; Released: entered FAB line; In Process: active WIP; Completed: all steps finished; Cancelled: terminated before completion; On Hold: temporarily suspended.
Precise date and time when the wafer lot was released into the FAB line, including time zone. Used for detailed cycle time analysis and shift-level tracking.

Classification of the wafer start purpose: production (customer orders), engineering (process development), qualification (product validation), MPW (multi-project wafer), pilot (pre-production), or rework (reprocessing).
SAP work center code representing the initial processing area for this wafer start. Used for capacity planning and cost allocation.
Primary key for fab_facility
Self-referencing FK on fab_facility (parent_fab_facility_id)
Maximum number of wafers the facility can process in a calendar month.
Monthly greenhouse‑gas emissions expressed in kilograms CO₂ equivalent.
City where the facility is located.
ISO cleanroom classification of the facility's most stringent environment.
Current compliance standing with regulatory and industry standards.
Primary email address for facility communications.
Primary telephone number for facility communications.
Three‑letter ISO country code of the facility location.
Free‑form description of the facility's capabilities and role.
Date when the facility ceased operations (null if still active).
Average monthly energy consumption measured in megawatt‑hours.
Comma‑separated list of environmental certifications (e.g., ISO 14001).
Free‑text summary of key equipment types and models present.
Total usable floor area of the fab in square feet.
External business code or identifier used to reference the facility in enterprise systems.
Human‑readable name of the fabrication facility.
Category of the facility based on its primary function.
Date of the most recent compliance audit.
Geographic latitude of the facility (decimal degrees).
Current lifecycle stage of the facility.
Primary lithography technology employed at the fab.
Primary street address of the facility.
Secondary address information (suite, building, etc.).
Geographic longitude of the facility (decimal degrees).
Typical time window (e.g., weekly, monthly) allocated for planned maintenance.
Full name of the person responsible for overall facility management.
Scheduled date for the next compliance audit.
Any supplemental information or remarks about the facility.
Count of cleanroom spaces within the facility.
Total number of major equipment units (e.g., lithography, etch, deposition) installed.
Current operational condition of the facility.
Primary semiconductor process node supported (e.g., 7nm, 5nm).
Indicates whether the facility requires restricted (security‑cleared) access.
Comma‑separated list of safety certifications held (e.g., ISO 45001).
Number of production shifts operated per day.
Date when the facility began operations.
State or province of the facility location.
Total waste generated per month measured in metric tons.
Average monthly water usage in cubic meters.
Unique identifier for the FAB yield record. Primary key for this transactional yield capture event.

Foreign key linking to test.bin_definition. Business justification: Yield records track bin-level die counts (bin_1_die_count, bin_2_die_count, bin_3_die_count). Linking to bin_definition enables structured yield analysis by bin category, disposition rules, and yield

Foreign key linking to quality.control_plan. Business justification: Fab yield records are collected under a specific control plan governing sampling rates and measurement criteria. IATF 16949 requires traceability from measurement records to the control plan. Quality
Reference to the FAB facility where this yield measurement was captured. Enables multi-site yield comparison and benchmarking.
Reference to the inspection or metrology equipment used to capture this yield measurement (e.g., KLA ICOS system). Enables equipment-specific yield correlation.
Reference to the photomask set used for lithography on this wafer. Enables correlation of yield to mask quality and OPC effectiveness.
Reference to the wafer lot to which this yield record belongs. Enables lot-level yield aggregation and genealogy tracking.
Foreign key linking to product.ic_catalog. Business justification: Enables Yield Dashboard to aggregate yields per IC catalog product.

Foreign key linking to supply.material_master. Business justification: Yield excursion analysis by material source is a standard semiconductor fab practice. Linking fab_yield_record to material_master enables supplier quality scorecards, yield-by-material-lot correlation

Foreign key linking to quality.nonconformance_report. Business justification: When fab yield records trigger yield excursions (yield_excursion_flag=true), a nonconformance report is initiated. Direct FK enables yield excursion management workflow — quality engineers must trace

Reference to the specific wafer for which yield is being recorded. Links to the wafer master record in the fabrication domain.
Reference to the process route or recipe used for this wafer. Links yield outcomes to specific process flows.

Foreign key linking to test.test_program. Business justification: Yield records are generated by specific test programs. Knowing which test program produced the yield data is essential for yield learning, test program optimization, and correlating test program versi

Foreign key linking to quality.quality_spec. Business justification: Fab yield records are measured against quality specs defining control limits and yield targets. SPC monitoring and yield excursion detection require direct reference to the governing quality spec. Thi

Foreign key linking to product.sku. Business justification: SKU-level yield reporting is the primary input for bin split analysis, SKU availability commitments, and pricing decisions. fab_yield_record has ic_catalog_id but not sku_id — SKU-level yield (by spee

Foreign key linking to design.tapeout. Business justification: Yield records are analyzed per tapeout version to distinguish design-induced vs. process-induced yield loss. Design and process teams jointly review yield by tapeout for re-spin decisions and design i
